title: The 'I Don't Deserve' Tax on Every Receiving Moment
subtitle: A reading on the self-sabotage pattern that levies a hidden charge on money, success, and receiving
excerpt: A reading on the self-worth-and-money pattern most people have and most don't name. Covers how the 'I don't deserve' belief operates as an invisible tax on receiving — creating hesitation, deflection, or unconscious sabotage whenever more arrives.

practice.whenToUse: Read when money arrives and something makes you shrink from it, deflect the compliment, give it away too fast, or feel vaguely wrong about having it.

content.text: Most people have a self-worth baseline that doesn't fully match the financial level they want. They want more, intellectually. But somewhere underneath that, they don't quite believe they're the kind of person who gets to have it. That gap is the 'I don't deserve' tax.
What the tax looks like in practice

The tax shows up differently for different people. Some deflect the compliment or downplay the success — 'oh it wasn't that much really.' Some give money away almost as soon as it arrives, before it can settle. Some set prices lower than the work warrants, or accept less than was offered without questioning it. Some feel a kind of ambient guilt around having money at all.
These aren't random. They're the tax being collected. Every receiving moment runs through the filter of 'do I deserve this?' and gets reduced accordingly.

The 'I don't deserve' pattern usually has a specific origin: a message received in childhood about what you were worth, what people from your background were allowed, or what it meant to want too much. Sometimes it's explicit. More often it's absorbed through what was modelled around you, or through the times you reached for more and it was taken back or made uncomfortable.
The belief is old. It's not accurate about who you are now or what you're capable of. But it runs until it's specifically addressed.

The practices that address this most directly are the worthiness and self-worth sections of the library: the tapping scripts for receiving guilt, the energy statements for claiming what's yours, the journal prompts for examining what 'deserving' means in your specific story. The pattern runs deep enough that a single practice session won't fully clear it. The 84-day arc addresses it at multiple points in sequence.
